transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright, I think we have everyone — or close enough. Jordan I know isn't on but I'll make sure he gets the notes. Welcome everyone to the Project Meridian kickoff. Super excited to have DataBridge and CloudPeak in the same room, so to speak. Quick housekeeping: I'll be sending out action items after this call, so don't feel like you need to take furious notes. Tom, you want to kick us off with the scope overview?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure, thanks Sarah. So, at a high level — Acme's current integration layer is an HL7 v2 MLLP-based system, which was totally fine ten years ago but it's getting expensive to maintain and it can't really scale to the API surface area you're going to need for the network partnerships you're planning, right? The new stack is FHIR R4, REST-based, much more interoperable. Our job at DataBridge is to build the translation and routing layer that connects your source systems to the new API surface. We handle the data mapping, the transformation logic, the error handling. CloudPeak is standing up the compute and network environment that this all runs on.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Tom, can you speak to the data mapping piece specifically? That's the part I've heard the most concern about internally. We have a lot of custom fields in the legacy system.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah, absolutely. So the data mapping document is going to be the critical artifact here. It defines, field by field, how each source data element maps to the corresponding FHIR resource — patient demographics, encounter records, diagnostic results, the works. Marcus, your team is going to need to review and sign off on that document because you know the source system better than anyone. Once that's approved, we build against it. If the mapping changes after we've started building, we're in rework territory, so getting that right upfront is really important.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Understood. What does the first draft look like timeline-wise?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: I'll have a preliminary version to you by January 15th. That gives your team a week to review, and we're targeting approval by January 22nd. That's the date we've built into our schedule.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Perfect. I'll put that in the action items. January 15th, Tom delivers data mapping v1. January 22nd, Acme review complete.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: From our side, CloudPeak is assuming mapping approval by January 22nd as the trigger to finalize our environment config. Jordan has already started setting up the base network topology, but the specific compute allocation and security group rules depend on the final integration architecture. So the sooner we know what the middleware stack looks like, the better.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Right, I'll make sure you have the architecture doc alongside the mapping doc. You'll need the deployment footprint to finish the CloudFormation templates.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Exactly. Jordan's been waiting on that to finalize the security group rules.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Great. Okay, so phasing. Diana, this was something we talked about in the pre-call last week — do you want to walk through the rationale?

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Sure. Our recommendation going in was a phased approach. Phase 1 is read-only FHIR endpoints — patient lookup, encounter history, that kind of thing. We run that in parallel with the legacy system for 30 days, validate the data quality, and then Phase 2 brings write endpoints online and we start the cutover. The reason is risk management — if something goes wrong in Phase 1 we haven't touched any production write paths.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That makes sense from a risk perspective. We can build to that. It's a little more work on our side because we're essentially delivering two integration surfaces instead of one, but it's very doable.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: And from a contracting standpoint the phased approach is what the current SOW reflects, so we're aligned there.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: CloudPeak can accommodate either approach. Phased means we bring up the read environment first, validate, then cut over the write environment. Slightly more coordination but manageable.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright, so we're aligned on phased migration. Phase 1 read-only, Phase 2 write, 30-day validation window in between. I'll document that as a decided item.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: One thing I want to flag — we have about fifteen custom lab result fields in the legacy system that aren't in any standard FHIR profile. Tom, I'll send you a document that describes those. They're the ones I'm most nervous about in the mapping.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Please do, that's really helpful. We'll figure out whether we extend the standard profile or use a contained resource. There are a few options.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll send that today.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus sends Tom the custom lab fields doc today, January 6th.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Sarah, on the milestone side — the SOW has four billing milestones tied to deliverable acceptance. I want to make sure we're aligned on what 'acceptance' means for the mapping doc. Is that a formal sign-off email from Acme, or is there a formal approval workflow?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good question. Diana, what's the threshold on that?

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: For the mapping doc specifically, I'd say a written email from Marcus or Linda confirming the review is complete is sufficient. We don't need a formal DocuSign or anything. But it does need to be explicit — no implied approvals.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: That works. I'll note that in the project tracker. Tom, same question for the architecture package — is that the same bar?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah, for us a written confirmation from Sarah that the architecture doc is accepted is the trigger. We just need something we can point to.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Noted. I'll be the accepting party on the architecture package. Marcus and Linda on the mapping doc.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: One more thing on the mapping — I want to set expectations about how we handle null and default values. The legacy system has a lot of fields that are technically required by FHIR but populated inconsistently in source. We're going to need a documented policy on how to handle those. Marcus, is there someone on your team who owns data governance?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That would be Linda for the clinical data side. For anything administrative — demographics, insurance — that's my team.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Okay. So in the mapping doc I'll flag the fields with data quality issues and we'll need decisions from both of you. I'll probably set up a separate tab in the spreadsheet just for those.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: — sorry, looks like she hasn't joined yet.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Linda's not on yet — she said she might be a few minutes late. Tom, keep going.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure. So alongside the mapping, I also want to discuss error handling strategy. When a message comes in from the legacy system and we can't map a field — either it's missing required data or it's in a format we don't recognize — what's the expected behavior? Do we reject the transaction? Log and continue? That policy needs to be locked down early.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: From an operations standpoint, I'd prefer we log and continue for non-critical fields, and hard-reject for anything that would compromise patient safety — medication orders, allergy records. But we should define the tiers.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's exactly what we'd recommend. We'll build a criticality tier into the mapping doc — Tier 1 is hard-reject, Tier 2 is log-and-flag, Tier 3 is best-effort. You'll need to validate the tier assignments.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: I want to make sure compliance is comfortable with any log-and-continue behavior on clinical records. Tom, can you include a brief rationale column in the mapping doc explaining why each Tier 2 field got that rating? If I get questions from our compliance team I want to be able to defend the choices.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Absolutely, that's a good idea regardless. We can add that.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: From an infrastructure standpoint, we're going to need to know the expected message volume. Our compute sizing depends on peak throughput. Do you have numbers on that, Marcus?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: We're running about 40,000 ADT messages a day at peak, roughly 2,000 per hour during business hours. Lab results are another 15,000 a day. I can send you a more detailed breakdown — we have six months of logs.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: That would be really helpful. Jordan is doing the capacity planning — can you send it to both of us?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sure, I'll pull that report today.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus sends volume report to Priya and Jordan today.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: On the FHIR side, I want to confirm we're targeting R4 and not STU3. There are organizations still on STU3 and the resource structures are different enough that it matters.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: R4. That was specified in the RFP.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good. Just wanted it on the record. Aisha will build the test suite against R4 conformance.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Priya, quick question on the environment — are we talking AWS, Azure, something else?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: AWS. Jordan has been working in us-east-1. We'll also set up a disaster recovery footprint in us-west-2, but the primary stack is us-east-1.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's consistent with what we scoped. Our middleware stack runs in containers — Fargate most likely. We'll need to align on VPC configuration and make sure our service can reach Acme's source systems.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Our internal systems are on-prem. We'll be providing a site-to-site VPN or direct connect for the integration layer to reach our HL7 feeds. IT is already engaged on that.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good. We'll need the network specs — IP ranges, port requirements — before we can finalize the deployment config. That should probably go to Jordan?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Yes, Jordan owns the network layer. You can copy me but send the technical specs to Jordan directly.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Marcus, can you get those network specs to Jordan by January 13th? That gives CloudPeak a week before the mapping review starts.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll check with IT but I think that's doable.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. Action item — Marcus coordinates with IT to send network specs to Jordan by January 13th.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Tom, on our side — once the mapping is approved, what does the build timeline look like? Just so I can update the project schedule.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Mapping approved January 22nd, we finalize the architecture package by the 29th. The core transformation logic we can have coded and unit-tested within about a week of mapping approval. Integration testing — that's Aisha's domain — maybe five to seven business days assuming no major surprises. Then UAT with Acme's team, which I'd suggest is at least five business days. That gets us to roughly February 10th, which gives us a few days buffer before February 14th.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's tight but workable. Aisha, you're not on the call but I'll confirm this with her directly. Kevin, note that as the indicative schedule.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Got it. I'll update the tracker.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Can we talk about the UAT process for a moment? Who from Acme's side is owning that?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That would be Linda and me, along with a couple of people from the clinical informatics team. We have a standard UAT checklist we use for integration projects. I'll share it with Tom and Aisha so they know what we'll be testing.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That would be really helpful. The more we know about what you're going to test, the more we can make sure our integration test suite covers the same scenarios.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus shares Acme UAT checklist with Tom and Aisha by January 20th.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Is there a rollback plan? If we cut over on February 14th and something goes wrong, what's the path back to the legacy system?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. Because we're building a translation layer rather than replacing the source system, the legacy HL7 feed stays intact until we explicitly decommission it. A rollback is essentially just redirecting traffic back to the legacy path. That's a configuration change — probably less than an hour to execute.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: From our side, we'll have the legacy path in standby for at least 30 days post-cutover. Jordan will document the rollback procedure as part of the runbook.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Good. I want that rollback procedure reviewed and approved before we go live.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Noted. That goes on the pre-launch checklist.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: One more pre-launch item I want to raise — we'll need a HIPAA BAA in place between DataBridge and Acme before we can access any PHI, even in a test environment. Kevin, has legal started on that?

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: The BAA is in draft. I'll follow up with our legal team — I'd expect it to be ready within the week.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's a dependency on the test environment access. Kevin, can you target January 13th for the signed BAA?

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: I'll push for that. Our legal team is usually pretty fast on BAAs.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Thank you. Action item — Kevin targets signed BAA by January 13th.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Once the BAA is in place we can also get credentials for your staging environment so Aisha can start test harness development. Marcus, who manages access provisioning on your side?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's our IT security team. I'll connect you with them once the BAA is signed.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay, I think we're making good progress. Let me pause — Linda was supposed to join. Let me check the waiting room...

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Sorry, just joined — did I miss the data mapping discussion?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Hey Linda! Yeah, we covered it — Tom's delivering a draft on the 15th, we have until the 22nd to review. I'll make sure you're on the review thread.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Perfect, thank you. I just want to make sure I can check the ADT feed field names against the mapping — we had a discrepancy in the last migration project.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Good call, Linda. You and I can sync before we send our review back to Tom.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Linda, just so you're caught up — on the custom lab fields, we'll handle most of them as FHIR extensions. In the mapping doc I'll use a dedicated tab to list each non-standard field, the proposed extension URL, and the null-value handling policy. You'll be the primary reviewer for that section.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Understood. I'll flag anything that looks off on the ADT side too. In our last migration we had a problem where the sending system was populating PID-3 with an encounter ID instead of the MRN, and it caused downstream duplicate record issues. I want to make sure we catch anything like that early.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's exactly the kind of thing we need to know about. If you have documentation on known data quality issues in the source HL7 feeds, please send it along with the custom fields doc.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: I do have a document from the last migration audit. It's from 2022 but most of it is still applicable. I'll send that.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Great. Action item — Linda sends Tom the 2022 migration audit doc alongside the custom lab fields.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Linda, quick question from the infrastructure side — do you know if Acme's HL7 interface engine has any hard limits on concurrent connections? We're going to be standing up a listener on our end and I want to make sure we don't saturate your side.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Honestly I'm not sure of the exact number. We're running Rhapsody as the interface engine. I'll check with IT.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Rhapsody, okay. We've integrated with Rhapsody before. Standard MLLP listener, right?

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Correct. MLLP on port 5000 in our staging environment. Production is 6100 but you won't be touching that until cutover.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Good. Jordan will need those port details for the security group config.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I'll make sure Jordan has them.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright. We should talk about the testing environment setup. Tom, walk us through what you need from Acme to stand up your dev and test environments.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure. We need three things: one, a copy of the staging HL7 feed — ideally replaying a week of recent production messages with PHI scrubbed. Two, read access to the source system data dictionary so we can validate field populations. Three, a test FHIR endpoint to push our output to — we'll be running the FHIR validator against everything we produce.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: The staging feed I can arrange. The data dictionary — that's documentation that lives in our IT department. Linda, do you know who owns that?

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: It's managed by our informatics team. I can get it. It's about 400 pages but I'll send the relevant sections.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Any format is fine. PDF, Excel, whatever you have.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: And the FHIR test endpoint — Priya, is that CloudPeak's responsibility to stand up?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: We can stand up a HAPI FHIR server in the test environment for validation purposes. Jordan can have that ready by January 20th assuming the BAA is signed and we have network connectivity.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That works for our timeline.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Priya, action item — CloudPeak stands up HAPI FHIR test server by January 20th, contingent on BAA and network connectivity.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Sarah, I want to make sure we have a documented escalation path. If something is at risk and the project team can't resolve it, who's the next level up?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: For Acme, it's Diana. For DataBridge, Kevin — you're the account lead, right? So issues come to you?

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Correct. Tom is the technical lead, I own the account relationship. Anything that could affect scope, timeline, or contract goes through me.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: CloudPeak's escalation path is me, and above me is our VP of Delivery — but I'd rather handle things at my level unless it's truly a senior issue.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. Let's try to keep escalations rare. My goal is that Diana only hears about things that she actually needs to act on.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: That's appreciated, Sarah. But when in doubt, over-communicate. I'd rather get a heads-up that turns out to be nothing than find out about a problem a week after it became critical.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Understood.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: One more thing I want to put on the radar — change management. If Acme's requirements shift after the mapping is approved, we need a formal change process. Even small changes mid-build can cascade. I'd like to agree now on what constitutes a change request versus a bug fix.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: That's in the SOW actually. Section 4.3. But it's worth summarizing on this call.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Right. If the approved mapping document changes after sign-off, that's a formal change request which Kevin and Sarah process. A bug is when our implementation doesn't match the approved mapping. We need that distinction to be clear to everyone.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Understood. So we need to make sure our review comments are thorough before we sign off on the mapping, because once we do, any changes are CRs.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Exactly. Take the full review window. It's better to spend the time now than to do a CR in the middle of the build.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Noted. Alright, I think we need to move on — Kevin, did you have anything else on the contracting side?

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Just the payment milestone question we covered at the top. And one thing — the acceptance criteria for final delivery. When DataBridge completes the build and hands off, what does done look like?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: In the SOW it's a passing integration test suite, documented API endpoints, and a completed runbook. Plus Acme UAT sign-off.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And we'll add the rollback procedure to that list per Diana's earlier comment.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Good. I'll note that as an addendum to the acceptance criteria.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Alright. Good progress on that. Tom, let's spend a few minutes on the architecture diagram. Can you give us a verbal walkthrough of what the stack looks like end to end?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Sure. At a high level — Acme's Rhapsody engine sends HL7 messages over MLLP to a message broker we're running on AWS. We're using Amazon MQ for that, which gives us durability and replay capability. The broker feeds our transformation service, which is a containerized Java application that does the FHIR mapping. The output goes to a FHIR API layer — we'll be using HAPI FHIR server as the reference implementation, CloudPeak is hosting that. Then the FHIR API is exposed to the downstream network partners.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: For the CloudPeak piece — we're planning to put the HAPI FHIR server behind an API Gateway with WAF enabled. Jordan is handling the security layer. We'll also have CloudWatch monitoring on the whole stack.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's the right approach. I'll make sure the architecture doc captures the full stack so Priya and Jordan have a clear picture of the DataBridge components they need to connect to.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: What's the latency expectation for the transformation layer? Some of our clinical workflows require near-real-time updates.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: We're designing for sub-five-second end-to-end latency for a typical ADT message. Lab results may take longer depending on message size. We'll include latency SLAs in the architecture doc — but those numbers need Acme's validation.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Five seconds is fine for most workflows. The one exception is real-time ADT notifications to our ED system — those need to be under two seconds. That's maybe 10% of total volume.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Understood. We'll prioritize that message class in the routing layer. I'll flag that in the architecture doc.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. I think we've covered a lot of ground on the technical side. Kevin, anything else before we move to the timeline summary?

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: One thing from our side — for status updates, Kevin should be my first point of contact, and Tom for anything technical. I'll make sure Kevin is looped on all the scheduling.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Got it. Kevin for account stuff, Tom for architecture questions.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: I want to revisit the dependency chain one more time. My concern is that we have a lot of things that depend on the mapping approval, which depends on DataBridge delivering a clean v1. Tom, how confident are you in the January 15th delivery?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Very confident. We started the field analysis before this kickoff. Once Marcus sends the custom lab fields today I'll have everything I need. I'm planning to have a working draft to Aisha by the 13th for a quick internal review before it goes to Acme on the 15th.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Okay. And if there are significant issues in the Acme review — issues that require rework — what's the contingency?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: If there are minor issues, a day or two of turnaround. If there are significant structural issues with the mapping, we'd need to assess. That's why we're giving a full week for the review — I'd rather have feedback come in on January 18th with time to address it than January 22nd with no runway.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Marcus, if your team spots issues early in the review, flag them immediately rather than waiting to compile everything at the end?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Agreed. We'll review in sections and send rolling feedback.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: That's ideal. Rolling feedback lets us address things in parallel instead of all at once.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: While we're on contingencies — if the mapping slips and we don't have the architecture by the 29th, the February 14th date becomes very difficult. I want to be transparent about that. Ten business days is not a hard number I invented — it's based on the actual work Jordan and I need to do. There's not a lot of buffer built in.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Priya, what would you need to compress that if it came to it?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: If we get a draft architecture before it's finalized — even a 90% complete version — Jordan can start on the parts that won't change. That could save us two or three days. Tom, would that be possible?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. Once the mapping approval comes in I can have a preliminary architecture out within 24 hours. It won't be the final version but the network topology and security group requirements won't change much.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: That would help a lot. If we get a draft architecture by January 24th or 25th, Jordan can start. We'd absorb the risk of minor changes.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Tom, can you commit to sending a draft architecture to Priya and Jordan within 48 hours of mapping approval?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yes. 48 hours from approval, draft architecture goes out. Final version by the 29th.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. That's the parallel path. Action item — Tom sends draft architecture to CloudPeak within 48 hours of mapping approval. Final by January 29th.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: I want to flag something on the contract side. The SOW has a force majeure clause but it doesn't specifically address internal resource turnover. If DataBridge has staffing changes that affect delivery, how is that handled?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: DataBridge's commitment is to the deliverable, not to specific personnel. If I got hit by a bus — knock on wood — the engagement continues with whoever takes over. I'm not planning any changes, but you have my word that Aisha is fully ramped up and could carry the build if needed.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Good to know. Just want to make sure everyone is aligned on how that works.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah, one thing I want to document — what communication channels are we using? Right now I have email and Zoom. Is there a shared Slack workspace or project management tool?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yes. We set up a shared Slack channel — acme-databridge-shared. I'll send invites today to everyone on this call. For internal Acme discussions we have acme-meridian-ops. Tom, Kevin — shared channel for cross-team communication, please.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Works for me. I'll have Aisha join too.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Can CloudPeak be added to the shared channel as well? Or a separate one for CloudPeak-DataBridge coordination?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Let's keep it simple — one cross-team channel for now. I'll add you and Jordan to the acme-databridge-shared channel, we can rename it if needed.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Works for me.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: For sensitive architecture documents and mapping files, we should use email rather than Slack, just for proper audit trail and version control.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. Slack for coordination and quick questions. Email with the full distribution list for deliverables.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That makes sense. Sarah, can you send a summary of the distribution list after this call? I want to make sure I'm not missing anyone when I send the mapping review comments.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yes, I'll include it in the action item summary. Full DL will be everyone on this call plus Jordan and Aisha.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: One last thing from me — test data. When DataBridge is developing the transformation logic, are they using real patient data or synthetic data?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Synthetic data for development. We'll use de-identified production data for integration testing once the BAA is signed. Aisha generates the synthetic test data set from the message specs — she doesn't need real data to build the test cases.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Good. I just wanted to make sure we weren't shipping PHI to a development environment.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Never. Standard practice — PHI only in environments with full controls in place.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. I think we're approaching our end time. Let me move to the timeline summary.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Before you do — Priya, I want to make sure we're aligned on the CloudPeak deliverable dates so the project tracker is accurate. February 7th is your commitment for finalized environment, right?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: February 7th for a fully configured and validated environment, yes. That's assuming we have the final architecture doc by January 29th.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Got it. And DataBridge hands off the integration package — Tom, what's that date?

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: We're targeting February 10th for the complete integration package — transformation service, test suite, runbook. That gives Acme a few days for UAT before the 14th.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: February 10th. Got it. That's tight with UAT.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: It is tight. We may need to consider whether UAT can run in parallel with the final integration testing. Marcus, is that feasible?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: We can start UAT on a subset of scenarios before the full package is handed off, as long as the core workflows are stable. Let's see how the build goes before we decide.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. We'll revisit that closer to the date. Alright — timeline summary.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I want to confirm the cutover target date — February 14th. Is that still the plan?

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: February 14th is the board-approved date. We need to be live by then. There's budget approval tied to it.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Understood. Jordan and I will plan backwards from that. We'll need at least ten business days of environment configuration once we have the final architecture. So that means we need artifacts from DataBridge no later than January 29th.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: January 29th for the architecture package from us. Noted. That's aggressive but doable if the mapping approval comes in on the 22nd.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay, I'm noting that dependency — DataBridge needs the mapping approved by January 22nd to hit the January 29th architecture delivery. And CloudPeak needs architecture by January 29th to hit the February 14th cutover. That's the critical path.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: And if the mapping slips, the whole chain slips. I want everyone to internalize that. The mapping review is not a formality — it is the single most important milestone on this project.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Understood. We'll treat it that way.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Same on our end. January 15th is locked.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: I'll set up a weekly project status call starting next week. Tuesdays at 11 AM work for most people?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Works for me. Diana, you mentioned weekly status emails — would you want to join the status calls as well, or just receive the written update?

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Written update is fine unless something is at risk. No need for me to be on every call.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Perfect. Kevin, please send the recurring invite for Tuesdays 11 AM — include Tom, me, Marcus, Linda, Priya, and Jordan. Diana on a standing exception basis.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Will do. I'll have it out today.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: One last thing from CloudPeak — Jordan is going to need a point of contact at Acme's IT department for the network connectivity setup. Who should he reach out to?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That would be our network engineer — his name is Dev Rajan. I'll introduce Jordan and Dev over email today.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Great, thank you.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Action item — Marcus introduces Jordan to Dev Rajan today for network connectivity coordination.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: And I'll need Dev's contact too when it's time to set up the MLLP listener configuration.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll copy you on the introduction email.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: I just want to say — I know I came in late, but from what I've heard this sounds like a well-organized kickoff. I feel good about where this is heading.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Agreed. Good group. I think we can make February 14th if everyone stays in sync.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Cautiously optimistic. Jordan will be happy to finally see some movement on this after the pre-sales phase.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: I'll make sure Jordan gets the meeting notes and action items.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Great. Before I do the final summary, does anyone have anything else?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Just a comment — I want to make sure that during the build phase, DataBridge keeps us in the loop on any decisions that affect the mapping. If there are interpretation calls being made, I want to know, not just see them in the final product.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Fair ask. I'll flag any significant interpretation decisions as comments in the mapping doc and send a summary email when they come up.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's all I need.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Sarah, one more thing on the status updates — if anything moves to 'at risk,' I need to see it in that week's update, not the week after.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Understood. At-risk items go into the same week's update. No holding it for the next Friday.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Good.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Sarah, I want weekly status updates in my inbox. Keep them brief — three bullets is fine. What's on track, what's at risk, what needs my attention. If something slips to the at-risk column, I want to know before it becomes a crisis.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Absolutely. I'll send those every Friday.

transcript.speaker: Diana Osei
transcript.text: Thank you everyone. I have to drop off now — Sarah, talk soon.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Thanks Diana. Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: I'm going to need to drop too in about two minutes. Tom, let's connect after this call — I want to make sure the project schedule in our system matches what we aligned on today.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Yeah, send me a message and we can do a quick call this afternoon.

transcript.speaker: Kevin Park
transcript.text: Great. Thanks everyone. Good kickoff.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Thanks Kevin. Priya, are you okay if I send the final action item list as a follow-up? I want to make sure Jordan gets everything he needs.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Yes, please copy Jordan on everything from here on. He's the one doing the hands-on work and I want him fully informed.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Will do. Marcus, Linda — anything before we close?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'm good. Looking forward to seeing the mapping doc on the 15th.

transcript.speaker: Linda Marsh
transcript.text: Same. I'll block time that week for the review.

transcript.speaker: Tom Reyes
transcript.text: Appreciate it. I'll make sure it's in good shape before it goes out.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay, I think we've covered the major items. To summarize: Tom delivers data mapping v1 by January 15th. Acme reviews by January 22nd. Marcus sends custom lab fields doc today. DataBridge delivers architecture package by January 29th. CloudPeak finalizes environment config by February 7th. Cutover February 14th. Kevin and Tom are DataBridge contacts. I'll send the full action item list within the hour. Thanks everyone.
